headline: US and Iran begin historic direct talks in Pakistan as fragile ceasefire holds

sections.label: Situation Overview
sections.title: US-Iran Direct Talks Begin in Islamabad
paragraphs: The United States and Iran began face-to-face peace negotiations in Islamabad on Saturday afternoon, marking the first direct talks between the two countries since Iran's 1979 Islamic Revolution. US Vice President JD Vance is leading the American delegation alongside special envoy Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner, while Iranian Parliament Speaker Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf and Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi head Tehran's 70-person delegation.

The historic talks follow a fragile two-week ceasefire that began on Tuesday, halting US-Israeli air strikes on Iran after 43 days of conflict. Sources close to the mediation report the two sides have exchanged written texts during initial discussions, with Pakistan facilitating the negotiations that were originally planned as proximity talks but have evolved into direct face-to-face meetings.
key_facts: First US-Iran direct talks since 1979
Written texts exchanged during Saturday's negotiations
Two-week ceasefire began Tuesday after 43 days of war
Over 3,400 people killed in Iran, 1,500 in Lebanon during conflict
sections.australia_relevance: Australia has signed energy agreements with Singapore to maintain trade flows amid global supply disruptions caused by the conflict.

sections.label: Background
sections.title: Six-Week War Triggered by February Strikes
paragraphs: The current conflict began on February 28 when US and Israeli forces launched coordinated strikes on Iran, reportedly killing Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei. Iran retaliated by closing the Strait of Hormuz and launching hundreds of drones and ballistic missiles at Israel and US military bases across the Gulf region, including attacks on facilities in Bahrain, Jordan, Kuwait, Qatar, Saudi Arabia and the UAE.

The war has effectively shut down the world's most critical oil shipping route, with the Strait of Hormuz handling approximately 20% of global oil supplies before the conflict. Iran has faced accusations of attacking civilian infrastructure in Azerbaijan, Oman and Turkey, claims Tehran denies as false-flag operations.
key_facts: War started February 28 with US-Israeli strikes on Iran
Iran closed Strait of Hormuz, disrupting 20% of global oil supply
Hundreds of Iranian missiles and drones launched at regional targets
Multiple Gulf states hit by Iranian strikes on military bases
sections.australia_relevance: Australia imports roughly 90% of its liquid fuel, making it directly vulnerable to global oil price disruptions from the conflict.

sections.label: Diplomacy
sections.title: Competing Peace Plans as Lebanon Strikes Continue
paragraphs: Iran has presented a 10-point peace plan calling for guaranteed war termination and control over the Strait of Hormuz, while the US has countered with a 15-point proposal focused on restricting Iran's nuclear program and reopening the strait. Tehran insists any deal must include ending Israeli strikes on Hezbollah in Lebanon, while Washington and Tel Aviv maintain Lebanon is not covered by the ceasefire.

Despite the talks proceeding, Israel continued bombing southern Lebanon on Saturday, killing at least three people. Iran's Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi warned Tehran entered negotiations with "deep distrust" and was prepared to retaliate if attacked again, while President Trump posted on social media that Iran has "no cards" except temporary extortion through the Strait of Hormuz.
key_facts: Iran's 10-point plan seeks Hormuz control and war guarantee
US 15-point proposal focuses on nuclear restrictions
Lebanon ceasefire remains major sticking point
Israel continues Lebanon strikes despite Iranian objections
sections.australia_relevance: Australian government fuel reserves currently stand at 36 days of petrol, 34 days of diesel and 32 days of jet fuel - the largest stockpile in 15 years but still below international emergency requirements.

sections.label: Economy & Energy
sections.title: Oil Prices Fall But Remain Elevated
paragraphs: Oil prices dropped following the ceasefire announcement, with Brent crude falling 13% to $94.80 per barrel and US WTI crude declining over 15% to $95.75. However, prices remain well above pre-conflict levels of around $70 per barrel, with analysts forecasting potential spikes to $180-200 per barrel if disruptions continue through late April or June.

Australian petrol prices fell for the first time since the war began, dropping 5% to A$2.40 per litre in the week ending Sunday, though still about one-third higher than early March levels. Diesel prices rose to A$3.13 per litre, the highest on record since 2006, while analysts warn Australian consumers could face price jumps of 40 cents per litre if the conflict escalates.
key_facts: Oil fell 13-15% after ceasefire but remains $25 above pre-war levels
Australian petrol down 5% to A$2.40/L but still 33% above March
Diesel at record A$3.13/L, highest since 2006
Potential A$0.40/L increase if conflict worsens
sections.australia_relevance: Australia faces potential fuel supply shortages with only 26 days of usual petrol demand, 25 days of diesel and 20 days of jet fuel available if emergency protocols are activated.

sections.label: What to Watch
sections.title: Ceasefire Under Strain as Shipping Resumes
paragraphs: Only 12 ships have transited the Strait of Hormuz since the ceasefire began, compared to the usual 100 per day, with three Chinese-flagged oil tankers passing through on Saturday. Iran has floated charging transit fees, an idea widely rejected by the US and regional partners. Trump announced the US is "clearing out" the Strait of Hormuz though details remain unclear.

The talks could continue for up to 15 days according to Iran's Supreme National Security Council, while separate Israel-Lebanon negotiations are scheduled to begin in Washington on Tuesday. European airports warn of systemic jet fuel shortages within three weeks if Hormuz shipping doesn't resume significantly, adding urgency to the diplomatic efforts.
key_facts: Only 12 ships transited Hormuz since ceasefire vs usual 100/day
Iran considering transit fees, rejected by US and allies
Talks may continue up to 15 days
Israel-Lebanon talks set for Tuesday in Washington
sections.australia_relevance: Singapore and Australia have signed agreements to diversify crude oil supply origins as the region adapts to ongoing Middle East supply disruptions.
